research_topic: Recursive autonomous dev-loop plugin with council/tribunal methodology

claims.claim: Ralph Wiggum loop pattern (fresh context per iteration, git as memory layer) is production-tested and widely adopted across multiple implementations
claims.evidence_summary: Claude cites 7+ Ralph implementations including official Anthropic plugin. OpenAI identifies Ralph as foundational pattern for recursive dev-loops. Multiple GitHub repos demonstrate ecosystem adoption.

claims.claim: The edit-test-debug loop is the core pattern across all autonomous coding agents (Devin, SWE-Agent, OpenHands, Aider, AutoCodeRover)
claims.evidence_summary: All three researchers independently document identical loop structure: Plan -> Implement -> Test -> Evaluate -> [Pass: Commit | Fail: Debug]. Claude provides architectural comparison table. Gemini emphasizes ReAct (Reason-Act-Observe) as fundamental primitive.

claims.claim: Fresh context per iteration (Ralph pattern) prevents context pollution and hallucination accumulation in long-running loops
claims.evidence_summary: Claude recommends fresh context approach for dev-loop plugin, contrasting with accumulated context approach (Devin/OpenHands). Identifies context window limitations as practical constraint.

claims.claim: Constrained tool interfaces (like SWE-Agent's ACI) are more reliable than raw shell access
claims.evidence_summary: Gemini emphasizes ACI as 'single most impactful design decision' from SWE-Agent research, detailing specific commands (search, open, edit, test). OpenAI recommends similar structured tool interface. Both note constrained action spaces reduce hallucination errors.

claims.claim: Automated testing feedback loop is the most reliable signal for code quality and agent iteration
claims.evidence_summary: All three researchers identify test pass/fail as primary driver of autonomous improvement. Claude recommends 40% weight in composite scoring. Gemini emphasizes test output parsing as critical. OpenAI notes binary pass/fail signal plus detailed error logs.

claims.claim: LLM Council/Tribunal pattern with 3 independent models reduces error probability from p to ~3p²(1-p) when p<0.5
claims.evidence_summary: Claude cites Karpathy's LLM Council as foundational. Gemini provides mathematical proof of error reduction via majority voting. Both reference academic validation (2024 MIT study on Debating LLMs).

claims.claim: Anonymous tribunal reviews (anonymized LLM identities) prevent favoritism bias in multi-model voting
claims.evidence_summary: Claude attributes this insight to Karpathy's LLM Council design. Gemini mentions Delphi Method's anonymity property as directly applicable. Both recommend anonymization for peer review phase.

claims.claim: Simple majority voting captures most gains of multi-agent debate with lower cost than extensive debate rounds
claims.evidence_summary: Claude cites 2025 ACL paper 'Voting or Consensus?' finding that majority voting accounts for most observed gains. OpenAI recommends 2/3 majority for practical implementation. Both suggest full debate only for high-stakes decisions.

claims.claim: Claude Code sandboxing (bubblewrap on Linux, seatbelt on macOS) enables 84% reduction in permission prompts while maintaining security isolation
claims.evidence_summary: Claude documents Anthropic's sandboxing model with OS-level primitives, two-boundary isolation (filesystem + network). States open-source sandbox-runtime available. No contradictions from other researchers, likely not their focus area.

claims.claim: 99% composite quality threshold is extremely ambitious; industry standard is 80% test coverage, most production systems operate below that
claims.evidence_summary: Claude explicitly warns 99% is 'extraordinarily ambitious' with risk of infinite loops. OpenAI recommends making threshold configurable. Both note gap between threshold target and real-world expectations.

claims.claim: Convergence detection (grade improvement < 1% for 2-3 consecutive iterations) is more efficient escape hatch than max iterations alone
claims.evidence_summary: All three researchers recommend convergence detection as primary termination mechanism. Claude specifies <0.001 delta for 3 iterations. OpenAI suggests 1% minimum improvement. Gemini emphasizes in multi-layer termination strategy.

claims.claim: Multi-layer termination strategy required: success threshold, convergence, budget, max iterations, user interrupt, stuck detection
claims.evidence_summary: Claude proposes 6-layer approach explicitly. OpenAI lists 7 strategies including state machine approach. Gemini details 8 termination conditions with trigger mechanisms. All emphasize non-negotiable max iterations safety net.

claims.claim: Exponential Moving Average (EMA) with learning rate 0.1 is lightweight effective approach for tracking skill/model performance over time
claims.evidence_summary: Claude specifies formula: success_rate = 0.9*old + 0.1*new. OpenAI recommends similar approach. Gemini provides identical algorithm for updating tribunal model weights. All cite as proven RL pattern.

claims.claim: Weighted voting in tribunal should use EMA-adjusted weights favoring historically more reliable models
claims.evidence_summary: All three researchers independently recommend dynamic weighting. Claude specifies weights adjusted via EMA. OpenAI includes in critical design decisions table. Gemini details as core tribunal voting mechanism.

claims.claim: Composite quality score should weight specification compliance (40%), correctness/tests (30%), security (15%), code quality (10%), completeness (5%)
claims.evidence_summary: Claude provides weighted formula. OpenAI suggests slightly different split: Tests 35%, Coverage 20%, Lint 15%, Types 15%, Security 15%. Gemini recommends: Spec 40%, Correctness 30%, Security 15%, Completion 5%, Quality 10%. Close consensus.

claims.claim: SWE-Agent achieved 12.29% pass@1 on SWE-bench using structured ACI interface instead of raw shell
claims.evidence_summary: Claude cites exact benchmark number. OpenAI and Gemini reference SWE-Agent as state-of-the-art baseline. Claude notes 72% resolution on SWE-Bench Verified with Claude Sonnet.

claims.claim: Tiered permission model required: L0 (read-only implicit), L1 (safe write default), L2 (network/VCS per-session), L3 (deployments/credentials always approval)
claims.evidence_summary: OpenAI proposes exact tier structure. Gemini details identical 4-layer model with specific examples. Both emphasize L3 (deployments, API keys) requires explicit per-action approval.

claims.claim: Git branch operations must be blocked; agent restricted to designated branch only
claims.evidence_summary: Claude specifies 'branch operations blocked' per design. OpenAI lists 'branch create/switch BLOCK'. Gemini emphasizes 'branch locking to current branch is essential' in safety section.

claims.claim: MCP (Model Context Protocol) enables dynamic tool discovery and runtime plugin installation without restarts
claims.evidence_summary: Claude details November 2025 MCP spec additions (authorization, async, enterprise governance). OpenAI recommends MCP for standardized tool interfaces. Gemini suggests MCP as practical foundation for plugin architecture.

claims.claim: LLM-as-Judge pattern used as supplementary evaluator provides semantic correctness checking beyond automated tests
claims.evidence_summary: Claude references CodeVisionary Framework with 2-stage evaluation. OpenAI recommends LLM judge for high-stakes evaluations. Gemini specifies LLM-as-Judge for readability, idiomatic style, architectural soundness scoring.

claims.claim: Multi-dimensional grading metrics required: test coverage (80%+ standard), lint (0 errors), type safety (0 errors), complexity, duplication, security (0 critical/high)
claims.evidence_summary: Claude provides detailed table of metrics with thresholds. OpenAI lists specific tools (SonarQube, CodeClimate). Gemini specifies measurement methods for each category. All recommend multi-tool approach.

claims.claim: Composite grading formula should normalize each metric to 0-1 scale and combine with configurable weights
claims.evidence_summary: Claude provides explicit formula: grade = sum(metric * weight). OpenAI recommends same approach. Gemini specifies normalized 0-1 scale with total Q = 0.99 threshold. All note weights should be project-configurable.

claims.claim: Test pass rate should be weighted heavily (30-40%) in composite score as primary correctness signal
claims.evidence_summary: Claude specifies 40% for test pass rate. OpenAI recommends 35%. Gemini suggests highest priority. All emphasize as foundation of automated evaluation.

claims.claim: Agent performance metrics should be tracked via bandit algorithms (UCB1, Thompson sampling) for optimal skill/model selection
claims.evidence_summary: OpenAI identifies skill selection as multi-armed bandit problem. Gemini details UCB1 algorithm with exploration bonus. Both recommend over simple averaging for balancing exploitation/exploration.

claims.claim: Event sourcing architecture (logging all thoughts, actions, observations) is critical for debugging, reproducibility, and meta-learning
claims.evidence_summary: Claude recommends structured JSON logging. Gemini details OpenDevin's EventStream as best-practice model, emphasizing for replay and meta-analysis. OpenAI mentions audit logging without full event stream emphasis.

claims.claim: Oscillation detection via code state hashing can identify when agent undoes its own work repeatedly
claims.evidence_summary: OpenAI recommends tracking history via code hashes. Gemini specifies Git commit SHA or AST hash approach. Both identify stuck-in-loop as common failure mode.

claims.claim: Docker sandbox with non-root user, read-only filesystem (except /workspace), no network access is recommended execution environment
claims.evidence_summary: Claude references Claude Code sandboxing. OpenAI recommends container isolation. Gemini specifies Docker configuration details plus mentions gVisor/Firecracker for stricter isolation.

claims.claim: Principal of least privilege: agent should have access only to workspace directory, read config, write source/test files, execute test/build tools
claims.evidence_summary: OpenAI details specific access rights. Gemini emphasizes least privilege principle. Both recommend minimal permissions as security foundation.

claims.claim: Git push requires explicit user approval; git commit on current branch can be auto-allowed per design
claims.evidence_summary: Claude's permission model approves git commit on current branch but blocks push. OpenAI specifies identical requirements. Gemini emphasizes branch locking as essential safety feature.

claims.claim: Maximum iteration limit (e.g., 50) is non-negotiable safety net to prevent infinite loops
claims.evidence_summary: Claude specifies 50 iterations as hard limit. OpenAI recommends 10. Gemini emphasizes as critical backstop. All researchers unanimously recommend despite convergence detection.

claims.claim: Cost/token budget circuit breaker required; track usage per LLM provider and enforce hard limit
claims.evidence_summary: Claude warns multi-LLM tribunal cost compounds: 10 iterations * 3 LLMs = 30+ calls minimum. OpenAI includes cost-based circuit breaker. Gemini specifies integration with token APIs and cloud billing.

claims.claim: Time-based circuit breaker (wall-clock timeout) and user interrupt handling must save state to checkpoint immediately
claims.evidence_summary: OpenAI recommends timeout limits and graceful interrupt handling. Gemini details checkpoint system for long-running tasks. Both emphasize state persistence for resume capability.

claims.claim: Scope detection via keyword analysis, file count estimation, and cross-cutting concern heuristics should route small tasks to 'Tactic Mode' and large tasks to 'Strategy Mode'
claims.evidence_summary: Gemini details scope detection heuristics and two-workflow approach. Tactic Mode: plan+tasks+execute. Strategy Mode: full spec elaboration. Other researchers don't focus on adaptive scope routing.

claims.claim: Agent should detect capability gaps, scaffold new plugins via tool-maker pattern, validate in quarantine sandbox, and dynamically register
claims.evidence_summary: Claude specifies self-extension via EvoAgentX/SICA research. OpenAI recommends tool-maker/tool-user pattern. Gemini details full lifecycle: detection -> scaffolding -> validation -> registration.

claims.claim: Plugin manifest should include name, version, entrypoint, parameters, permissions_required fields; follows VSCode extension model
claims.evidence_summary: Gemini provides example manifest JSON structure. Claude mentions MCP dynamic discovery but not specific manifest format. OpenAI discusses general plugin registry without schema detail.

claims.claim: Newly created plugins must pass constitutional review by LLM governor to check for malicious intent before activation
claims.evidence_summary: Gemini specifies constitutional review as validation step. Related to broader theme of safety constraints. Other researchers emphasize sandbox testing but not LLM governance of plugins.

claims.claim: SICA (Self-Improving Coding Agent) demonstrated 17% -> 53% accuracy improvement on SWE Bench Verified via self-modification of tool orchestration, not LLM weight updates
claims.evidence_summary: Claude cites SICA paper (arXiv:2504.15228, ICLR 2025 Workshop) with specific performance metrics. Key insight: agent improved itself through behavioral changes, not retraining. OpenAI and Gemini don't reference SICA.

claims.claim: Async/parallel API execution critical: total latency determined by slowest model (not sum of all) via asyncio or similar concurrency
claims.evidence_summary: OpenAI recommends asynchronous execution for tribunal. Gemini specifies asyncio pattern with detailed example code. Both emphasize for latency optimization in multi-LLM systems.
